questions.text: There are many _ still believe in superstitions. The correct relative pronoun to fill in the gap will be:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Which: This pronoun is typically used for things, not people.
What: This pronoun refers to things or ideas. While superstitions are ideas, they are being connected to people in this sentence.
Who: This pronoun refers to people. In this sentence, we're talking about people who believe in superstitions.
As: This is a conjunction, not a pronoun.

questions.explanation: Who: This pronoun refers to people. In this sentence, we're talking about people who believe in superstitions.

questions.text: If the theme of a text is NOT limited to a particular culture but is relevant to all times and places, it is said to have a/an:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A communal appeal would suggest that the theme is relevant only to a particular community or group.
An emotional appeal suggests that the theme evokes strong emotions in the reader but doesn't necessarily imply relevance across cultures or time periods.
A national appeal would imply that the theme is relevant only within a specific nation or country.
A theme with universal appeal transcends cultural boundaries and resonates with people from various backgrounds and time periods. Therefore, it is said to have a universal appeal.

questions.explanation: A theme with universal appeal transcends cultural boundaries and resonates with people from various backgrounds and time periods. Therefore, it is said to have a universal appeal.

questions.text: _ you take a taxi, you will still miss your plane. The most appropriate transitional device to fill in the blank will be:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "Even if" is used to introduce a condition that is unlikely or contrary to fact but is being considered for the sake of argument. In this case, it suggests that taking a taxi still won't prevent missing the plane.
"In case" is used to indicate preparation for a possible event or situation.
"Only if" is used to indicate that a particular condition must be met for something to happen.
"May be" is incorrect in this context as it doesn't convey the intended meaning of the sentence.

questions.explanation: "Even if" is used to introduce a condition that is unlikely or contrary to fact but is being considered for the sake of argument. In this case, it suggests that taking a taxi still won't prevent missing the plane.

questions.text: When he arrived, Zain noticed that the door was open. The underlined word is an example of
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Anaphoric reference, on the other hand, refers to a word or phrase that refers back to something mentioned earlier in the discourse.

Cataphoric reference refers to a linguistic device in which a word or phrase refers to something mentioned later in the discourse.The sentence, "he arrived" refers to Zain, who is mentioned later in the sentence.
Cross-reference typically involves referring from one part of a text to another part of the same text or to another text.
Lexical reference doesn't accurately describe the relationship between "he" and "Zain" in the sentence.

questions.explanation: Cataphoric reference refers to a linguistic device in which a word or phrase refers to something mentioned later in the discourse.The sentence, "he arrived" refers to Zain, who is mentioned later in the sentence.

questions.text: Unlike mammals, birds incubate their eggs outside their body. The underlined word means:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Break: This means to crack or fracture something. Breaking is not the intended purpose of incubation; it's about keeping the eggs warm and protected for hatching.
Hatch: This means for a young animal to emerge from its egg. This is the exact process that incubation helps facilitate.
Lay: This refers to the act of producing and depositing eggs. While birds do lay eggs before incubation, "incubate" refers to the process that happens after the eggs are laid.
Inhibit: This means to prevent or slow down something. Incubation is the opposite of inhibiting hatching; it's the process that encourages hatching.

questions.explanation: Hatch: This means for a young animal to emerge from its egg. This is the exact process that incubation helps facilitate.

questions.text: A traditional story sometimes popularly regarded as historical but NOT authenticated is termed as a/an:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Ballad: A ballad is a form of verse typically narrating a story of adventure or love. Ballads can be based on historical events or folklore, but again, the focus is on the poetic form and storytelling aspect.

Legend: A legend is a traditional story sometimes popularly regarded as historical but not authenticated. This definition perfectly matches the meaning we're looking for. Legends are often based on a grain of truth but have grown and been embellished over time.
Parable: A parable is a short fictional story that illustrates a moral or religious lesson. Parables are not typically historical in nature.

Epic: An epic is a long narrative poem about the deeds of legendary heroes. While epics can be based on historical events, they are often embellished and may not be fully authenticated. However, the focus is on the poem itself, not necessarily the historical accuracy.

questions.explanation: Legend: A legend is a traditional story sometimes popularly regarded as historical but not authenticated. This definition perfectly matches the meaning we're looking for. Legends are often based on a grain of truth but have grown and been embellished over time.

questions.text: Kiran comes home after her interview, runs to her bedroom, and slams the door. You can infer that_.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Her interview went well: There's no indication of excitement or positivity in her actions.

options.text: her interview did not go well.
options.explanation: Based on the details provided, the most likely inference is:B- Her interview did not go well
She is tired: Running and slamming a door aren't typical signs of tiredness.
She is excited: Slamming a door is not a typical way to express excitement.

questions.explanation: Based on the details provided, the most likely inference is:B- Her interview did not go well

questions.text: The tourists were mesmerized to see the beauty of K-2, and it was hard for them to take their eyes off it. Deduce the meaning of the underlined word:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Spellbound: This word means captivated or fascinated to the point of being speechless or motionless. This perfectly describes the tourists' reaction to K-2's beauty - they were so enthralled they couldn't look away.
Thrilled: While someone can be thrilled by something beautiful, it doesn't necessarily imply being unable to take their eyes off it.
Excited: Similar to thrilled, excitement doesn't necessarily involve being so captivated that you can't look away.
Shocked: Shock can be caused by beauty, but it's a much stronger emotion and wouldn't typically describe the experience of being mesmerized.

questions.explanation: Spellbound: This word means captivated or fascinated to the point of being speechless or motionless. This perfectly describes the tourists' reaction to K-2's beauty - they were so enthralled they couldn't look away.

questions.text: Being imperious, he expected everyone to obey him. The most appropriate word to replace the underlined word will be:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Peremptory: This word means urgent and demanding, often in a way that leaves no room for discussion. It aligns well with the idea of expecting obedience without question.
Short-tempered: This describes someone who gets angry easily, but it doesn't necessarily imply expecting obedience.
Wise: This describes someone with knowledge and good judgment. It's the opposite of what the sentence is trying to convey.
Abnormal: This means not following the usual standard or way of things. It's too general a term for the specific meaning of expecting obedience.

questions.explanation: Peremptory: This word means urgent and demanding, often in a way that leaves no room for discussion. It aligns well with the idea of expecting obedience without question.

questions.text: When the teacher was delivering his lecture, the students were all ears. The underlined expression can best be replaced with:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Observing carefully: This is a possible meaning, but "all ears" focuses specifically on listening, not general observation.
Involved whole heartedly: This emphasizes enthusiasm and engagement, but "all ears" is a more specific idiom about paying close attention through listening.
Taking notes: While taking notes can be a result of listening carefully, it's not the only way to pay attention. "All ears" emphasizes the act of listening itself.
Listening carefully: This directly captures the meaning of the idiom "all ears." It means paying close attention to what someone is saying by focusing your hearing.

questions.explanation: Listening carefully: This directly captures the meaning of the idiom "all ears." It means paying close attention to what someone is saying by focusing your hearing.

questions.text: My village is situated on the east bank of _ Indus. Which article will correctly fill in the blank?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "A" or "an" are indefinite articles used for unspecified things. In this case, we're referring to a definite geographical feature (the Indus River).
"A" or "an" are indefinite articles used for unspecified things. In this case, we're referring to a definite geographical feature (the Indus River).
The Indus is a specific river, and the sentence refers to a particular bank (east bank) of that specific river.
No article required wouldn't be appropriate because the sentence is referencing a particular river, not just any river in general.

questions.explanation: The Indus is a specific river, and the sentence refers to a particular bank (east bank) of that specific river.

questions.text: Marty, the class clown, made every one laugh with his funny gestures. The underlined part of the sentence is used as a/an:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Subject phrase: A subject phrase initiates the sentence and performs the action of the verb. "Marty" is the subject of the sentence, but the underlined part isn't the entire subject.
Object phrase: An object phrase receives the action of the verb. The sentence doesn't have an object, and "the class clown" doesn't receive the action of "made laugh."
Appositive phrase: An appositive phrase renames or clarifies the noun or noun phrase before it. "The class clown" renames and provides more information about "Marty," making it an appositive phrase.
Adjective phrase: An adjective phrase modifies a noun or pronoun. While "the class clown" describes "Marty," it does so in a way that renames it rather than simply providing an adjective description.

questions.explanation: Appositive phrase: An appositive phrase renames or clarifies the noun or noun phrase before it. "The class clown" renames and provides more information about "Marty," making it an appositive phrase.

questions.text: One must always be aware of _ duties. The correct possessive form of the indefinite pronoun will be:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: One is a singular indefinite pronoun, so the possessive form should also be singular.
His and hers are possessive pronouns for the masculine and feminine genders respectively. They wouldn't work in this sentence because "one" is gender-neutral.
His and hers are possessive pronouns for the masculine and feminine genders respectively. They wouldn't work in this sentence because "one" is gender-neutral.
Theirs is the possessive form of the plural pronoun "they." "One" is singular, so "theirs" wouldn't be appropriate.

questions.explanation: One is a singular indefinite pronoun, so the possessive form should also be singular.

questions.text: You _ see a doctor. Which one of the following modal verbs will best fill in the blank if it is intended to be a piece of advice?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Could: This suggests possibility, but it's not strong enough to convey the urgency of seeking medical attention.
May: This suggests permission, which isn't the intended meaning here.
Had better: This expresses strong advice, implying there could be negative consequences if the advice isn't followed. This is the most appropriate choice for a recommendation to see a doctor.
Might: This suggests possibility, similar to "could," but it's weaker and doesn't convey the urgency of "had better."

questions.explanation: Had better: This expresses strong advice, implying there could be negative consequences if the advice isn't followed. This is the most appropriate choice for a recommendation to see a doctor.

questions.text: They played well and won the game. The underlined verbs are _ and _ respectively.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The correct answer is A- finite, non-finite."Played" is a finite verb because it indicates the action of the subject "they" in a specific tense (past tense)."Won" is a non-finite verb because it does not indicate tense, person, or number; instead, it is in the base form without any inflection.
The correct answer is A- finite, nonfinite.
The correct answer is A- finite, nonfinite.
The correct answer is A- finite, nonfinite.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is A- finite, non-finite."Played" is a finite verb because it indicates the action of the subject "they" in a specific tense (past tense)."Won" is a non-finite verb because it does not indicate tense, person, or number; instead, it is in the base form without any inflection.

questions.text: After he had finished the work on time, Ali went out for a walk. Which one of the following will correctly replace the underlined part?
questions.subject: English

options.text: Having been finished the work on time
options.explanation: "Having been finished the work on time" is grammatically incorrect. "Been" is the past participle of "be" and doesn't work well with "finished" here.
"Having finished the work time" is awkward and grammatically incorrect. "Time" should not be the object of "finished."

options.text: Have been finishing the work on time
options.explanation: "Have been finishing the work on time" uses the present perfect continuous tense, which is not appropriate here. It suggests the action of finishing was ongoing, which isn't the case.

options.text: Had finished the work on time
options.explanation: "Had finished the work on time" clearly establishes that the work was completed before Ali went for a walk.

questions.explanation: "Had finished the work on time" clearly establishes that the work was completed before Ali went for a walk.

questions.text: I will be able to get in although I have no ticket. The underlined part of the sentence is a/an:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Noun clause: A noun clause functions as a noun in the sentence. It wouldn't be appropriate here because it's not replacing a noun; it's modifying the verb.
Adjective clause: An adjective clause modifies a noun or pronoun. There is no noun or pronoun being modified by the underlined part in this sentence.

Adverb clause: As mentioned previously, an adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb. In this case, "although I have no ticket" modifies the verb "will be able to get in" by expressing a condition.
Main clause: A main clause expresses a complete thought on its own.

questions.explanation: Adverb clause: As mentioned previously, an adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb. In this case, "although I have no ticket" modifies the verb "will be able to get in" by expressing a condition.

questions.text: Identify the phrase exemplifying the most appropriate order of adjectives.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: An expensive, new, imported car: "Expensive" is an opinion, "new" describes size/age, and "imported" refers to origin. This order isn't the most typical according to the "Royal Order."
A new, expensive, imported car: Similar to the first option, "new" doesn't perfectly fit the size/age category, and "expensive" comes before origin.
An imported, expensive, new car: "Imported" (origin) precedes "expensive" (opinion) which isn't ideal.
An imported, new, expensive car: This option follows the "Royal Order" most closely. "Imported" (origin) comes first, then "new" (possibly size/age), and finally "expensive" (opinion).

questions.text: Which one of the following sentences exemplifies future perfect continuous tense?
questions.subject: English

options.text: I will be shopping at 3 pm.
options.explanation: "I will be shopping at 3 pm" is future tense, not future perfect continuous. It talks about a specific future action at a set time.

options.text: I will be doing shopping at 3 pm.
options.explanation: "I will be doing shopping at 3 pm" is a bit awkward phrasing, but it still uses the future tense to talk about a single future event.

options.text: I will have shopping since 3 pm
options.explanation: "I will have shopping since 3 pm" makes no grammatical sense. "Shopping" cannot be used as a verb in this way.

options.text: I will have been shopping since 3 pm.
options.explanation: "I will have been shopping since 3 pm" correctly uses the future perfect continuous tense. It indicates that the shopping will have started before 3 pm and will likely continue past that time.

questions.explanation: "I will have been shopping since 3 pm" correctly uses the future perfect continuous tense. It indicates that the shopping will have started before 3 pm and will likely continue past that time.

questions.text: I moved to Lahore _ the age of ten. The correct preposition to fill in the blank will be:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: On is typically used for specific days or dates (e.g., on Monday, on December 1st). Age is not a specific date.
At is used for specific points in time, including age (e.g., at noon, at the age of ten). This makes it the most suitable choice here.
By often suggests a deadline or latest possible time (e.g., be finished by tomorrow). It doesn't fit the context of age.
Above refers to a position higher than something else. It's not relevant to age.

questions.explanation: At is used for specific points in time, including age (e.g., at noon, at the age of ten). This makes it the most suitable choice here.

questions.text: Suppose you are a member of the literary society of your college. The Principal has asked you to prepare a report on the condition of the college library. Your report should include the following:i. The condition of the library building, reading hall and seating capacityii. Number of books and magazines available for borrowingiii. State of the reference sectioniv. Recommendations for the purchase of new books and other improvements
questions.subject: English

questions.text: Change the following passage into future tenseI was sauntering along the path, around the back of the school, when I noticed a police car. It was zooming along the road, with its lights flashing. The police constables leapt out the panda car, then started banging on my neighbour’s front door. She opened the door, but looked surprised to see them. She asked them what they wanted. They apologized profusely and confessed that they had the wrong house.
questions.subject: English

questions.text: Read the given passages and answer any SIX questions including Question No.(i) appended to it. Note: Question No. (i) about summary writing is compulsory carrying 08 marks while the rest of the questions carry 04 marks eachPakistan is a land of love and hospitality. A land of spiritual endowment, it's also the resting place of many spiritual saints from all religions, be it the Sufi mystics of Islam, the Hindu Tiraths dating back to 3,000BC, the disciples of Buddha attaining "nirvana' buried under the remains of Gandhara civilisation, or Baba Guru Nanak, the founder of the Sikh religion. Pakistan has always whole-heartedly worked to present its historical sites - whether it is a church, Gurdwara, temple, mosque, museum, tomb, fort or shrine. Born in the foothills of the Himalayas, Buddhism found avid followers, supporters and patrons in Gandhara, the Land of Fragrance. Pakistan became the custodian of rich collections of sacred relics and superb specimens of art and architecture from Buddhist civilisation. For example, the Buddhist ruins of Taxila are priceless treasures of immense interest to Buddhists and researchers around the world. Taxila became a Centre of excellence which the first ever university was founded there in the 10th century BC. Since Punjab was the center of activities for Sikh Gurus, and later the political power base of the Sikhs, there are numerous sites in Pakistan that are sacred to the Sikh community. Among the sacred shrines is Gurdwara Darbar Sahib. Kartarpur, near Lahore. Thousands of pilgrims visit these places from all over the globe. Pakistan opened the Kartarpur Corridor to provide Indian Sikh pilgrims a visa-free access. Kartarpur Corridor was renovated and expanded as a symbol of goodwill of the people of Pakistan for the Sikh community and to pave way for interfaith harmony. All Gurdwaras and Sikh shrines in Pakistan have been declared sacred places and are meticulously maintained by the government. Pakistan is also a land of Saints and Saints who preached amity, peace and universal love. Their teachings promoted religious co-existence, communal harmony and tolerance in society. Perhaps that is why there has always been communal harmony in Pakistan. The shrines of Data Ganj Bakhsh Hajvery, Hazrat Abdullah Shah Ghazi, Hazrat Lal Shahbaz Qalandar, Sachal Sarmast and Hazrat Bahauddin Zakaria are worth a visit for spiritual solace and eternal satisfaction.QUESTIONS:i. Write down summary of the passage and suggest a suitable title.ii. What role did saints play in promoting a culture of tolerance?iii. Mention at least two examples which the writer has used to prove his claim that Pakistan is a land of spiritual endowment.iv. How is Pakistan a unique spectrum of cultural harmony?v. Mention any two factors that went into the rise of Gandhara Civilization?vi. What does the writer mean by ‘interfaith harmony’? How can it be further promoted?vii. Use the words that are bold and underlined (in the given text) in sentences of your own.viii. Have you visited any tourist resort in Pakistan? If yes, how was your experience? If no, which place will you like to visit first and why?
questions.subject: English

questions.text: Read the following poetic extract carefully and answer the questions appended to it:There are thousands to tell you it cannot be done, There are thousands to prophesy failure, There are thousands to point out to you one by one, The dangers that wait to assail you. But just buckle in with a bit of a grin, Just take off your coat and go to it; Just start in to sing as you tackle the thing That “cannot be done,” and you’ll do it.QUESTIONS:i. What message do you extract from the given lines?ii. What according to the poet should one do when people try to scare one from the dangers ahead? iii. Point out the use of personification in the given lines.ORThere was a time indeed they used to shake hands with their hearts: but that’s gone, son. Now they shake hands without hearts while their left hands search my empty pockets.QUESTIONS:i. What according to the poet is the difference between the way people welcomed each other in the past and the way they do these days?ii. Do you agree with the poet that modern man is more concerned with his gains rather than having any goodwill for others? Justify your answer.iii. Point out the use of alliteration in the lines
questions.subject: English

questions.text: Answer any ONE of the following parts of the question:i. Discuss the setting of one of the work of fiction you have read. Also point out its connection with the main action of the fiction.ii. A narrative work of literature depicts the desires and struggle of the characters. How does a writer bring home his message through his characters?
questions.subject: English

questions.text: There are FIVE errors of sentence structure, spelling, article, adverb and pronoun in the given paragraph. Rewrite the paragraph after correcting the errors. Underline the corrected words.“How anyone can forget the contribution of students in the struggle for Pakistan. The Quaid himself appreciated their role greatly. But once the separate homeland for Muslims of the Sub-continent became a reality, he advised them to focus full on their studies. He know that the nations that had made education their priority they had touched the heights of success.
questions.subject: English

questions.text: Use the following idiomatic expressions in the blanks to complete the sense of the passage.i. cold shoulderii. eye to eyeiii. all earsiv. second to nonev. apple of everyone’s eyesSara loved school and always did her best in each class. Her work was_. When the teacher was talking, she was_. She was the _. Everyone liked her because she was a good friend as well. She never gave another child the _, even if they didn’t see _with her on every matter.
questions.subject: English

questions.text: Change the following conversation into direct form of narration:Mr. Ahmed asked Mr. Asif why he had not come the day before yesterday. Mr. Asif replied that he had been down with cough, sore throat and fever. Mr. Ahmed asked him if he had visited a doctor. He respectfully replied that he had not visited the doctor. He added that instead he took some home-made remedies.
questions.subject: English
